coredata.dc:title: Bio-waste derived zero-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dot-doped Zinc Manganese Oxide (ZnMn₂O₄) based symmetric supercapacitors with an ultra-high energy density and exceptional cycling stability in neutral electrolytes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Nanda, Om Priya
Badhulika, Sushmee
coredata.dc:description: Supercapacitors based on neutral electrolytes provide advanced energy storage solutions with enhanced charge storage and rapid charge/discharge capabilities. This study presents a novel approach by incorporating bio-waste-derived zero-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into Zinc Manganese Oxide (ZnMn₂O₄) (ZMO) to develop CQD-doped ZnMn₂O₄ (CQD-ZMO). The CQD-ZMO is grown hydrothermally and the confirmation of formation is done using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Photoluminescence (PL) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as well. XRD confirms the polycrystalline nature of CQD-ZMO while PL showed green light emissions, indicating effective electronic transitions within it. Further, the confirmation of CQD doping in ZMO is elucidated via TEM with distinct distribution of 0D CQD spheres in ZMO nanosheets matrix with notable lattice fringes. Electrochemical measurements of CQD-ZMO with a 1 M Na₂SO₄ neutral electrolyte reveal a high specific capacitance of 611 mF/cm2 at 2 mA/cm2, demonstrating its exceptional charge storage capability. Additionally, a symmetric supercapacitor device fabricated using CQD-ZMO achieves an impressive energy density of 0.079 mWh/cm2 at a power density of 2.46 mW/cm2 within a voltage window of 1.2 V. The device also shows remarkable cycling stability, retaining 95.2 % of its capacitance over 5000 cycles at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. These findings highlight the CQD-ZMO's potential to enhance energy storage efficiency and stability in advanced supercapacitor applications, marking a significant step forward in the development of high-performance supercapacitors energy storage systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Zinc Manganese Oxide
Nanosheets
Energy density
Power density

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical measurements in three electrode configuration

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: S. B. acknowledges financial assistance from the Department of Science & Technology (DST) grant #DST/TDT/AM/2022/420.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pictorial representation of step-by-step synthesis procedure for (i) ZMO, (ii) CQD and (iii) CQD-ZMO.
The variation of PL emission spectrum of (a) CQDs and (b) CQD-ZMO, CIE color mapping of (c) CQDs and (d) CQD-ZMO, (e) UV-Vi spectroscopy of CQD-ZMO, (f) XPS survey spectra of CQD-ZMO.

(a) XRD pattern of ZMO, (b) XRD pattern of CQD-ZMO, (c) High magnification (40,000×) FESEM image of ZMO at, (d) Higher magnification (50,000×) FESEM image of ZMO, (e) low magnification (15,000×) FESEM image of CQD-ZMO at 1 μm scale, (f) Higher magnification (30,000×) FESEM image of CQD-ZMO at 0.5 μm scale, (g) EDS of CQD-ZMO.

Low magnification TEM micrographs of CQD-ZMO (a) revealing 0D CQD with red spheres and ZM O phase with green line, (b) clear nanosheets with CQDs distributed, (c) magnified image of green portion from Fig. b, higher magnification TEM micrographs of CQD-ZMO (d) and (e), HRTEM micrograph with two CQD spheres marked i.e. 1 and 2 with distinct lattice fringes (f), moire pattern plotted from region (1) and (2), (g) SAED pattern of CQD-ZMO. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) CV curves of CQD-ZMO at different scan rates, (b) GCD curves of CQD-ZMO at different current densities, (c) Comparative Specific capacitances (d) Nyquist plot, (e) Bode plot, (f) Bode phase of all three electrodes i.e. CQD, ZMO and CQD-ZMO.

(a) CV curves at different scan rates, (b) GCD plots at different potential window, (c) Specific capacitances and Ohmic drop obtained from GCD (d) Nyquist plot, (e) Ragone plot, (f) Cyclic stability of the symmetric supercapacitor.
Electrochemical performance comparison of different QD and ZnMn2O4 based materials.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CQD: Carbon Quantum Dots, EMIBF4/PVDF-HFP: 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate/poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene GQD: graphitic quantum dots, PANI: Polyaniline, PPy: Polypyrrole, ZnO: Zinc Oxide, rGO: reduced graphene oxide, PVA: polyvinyl alcohol, KOH- Potassium Hydroxide.

head.ce:title: Bio-waste derived zero-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dot-doped Zinc Manganese Oxide (ZnMn₂O₄) based symmetric supercapacitors with an ultra-high energy density and exceptional cycling stability in neutral electrolytes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad, Hyderabad 502285, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad, Hyderabad, 502285, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercapacitors based on neutral electrolytes provide advanced energy storage solutions with enhanced charge storage and rapid charge/discharge capabilities. This study presents a novel approach by incorporating bio-waste-derived zero-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into Zinc Manganese Oxide (ZnMn₂O₄) (ZMO) to develop CQD-doped ZnMn₂O₄ (CQD-ZMO). The CQD-ZMO is grown hydrothermally and the confirmation of formation is done using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Photoluminescence (PL) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as well. XRD confirms the polycrystalline nature of CQD-ZMO while PL showed green light emissions, indicating effective electronic transitions within it. Further, the confirmation of CQD doping in ZMO is elucidated via TEM with distinct distribution of 0D CQD spheres in ZMO nanosheets matrix with notable lattice fringes. Electrochemical measurements of CQD-ZMO with a 1 M Na₂SO₄ neutral electrolyte reveal a high specific capacitance of 611 mF/cm2 at 2 mA/cm2, demonstrating its exceptional charge storage capability. Additionally, a symmetric supercapacitor device fabricated using CQD-ZMO achieves an impressive energy density of 0.079 mWh/cm2 at a power density of 2.46 mW/cm2 within a voltage window of 1.2 V. The device also shows remarkable cycling stability, retaining 95.2 % of its capacitance over 5000 cycles at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. These findings highlight the CQD-ZMO's potential to enhance energy storage efficiency and stability in advanced supercapacitor applications, marking a significant step forward in the development of high-performance supercapacitors energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid expansion of portable electronic devices and hybrid electric vehicles has significantly increased the demand for high-power energy storage solutions [1]. Supercapacitors, also known as electrochemical capacitors or ultracapacitors, have emerged as ideal candidates for energy storage due to their ability to provide higher specific power, longer cycle life, and faster charge–discharge processes compared to traditional batteries and fuel cells [2,3]. This has led to their widespread use in consumer electronics, memory backup systems, hybrid electric vehicles, and industrial power management. The growing need for green energy solutions and the concern over depleting fossil fuels have further intensified research into alternative energy storage technologies [1,4].

Among various energy storage devices, pseudocapacitors have gained attention due to their ability to combine high energy and power densities, offering a balanced solution for modern energy demands [5,6]. However, the development of high-performance electrode materials remains a challenge. Transition metal oxides (TMOs), such as RuO2, MnO2, and NiO, have shown promise as pseudocapacitive electrodes due to their high energy densities [7,8]. Nonetheless, their practical application is limited by their low electrical conductivity and structural instability during charge–discharge cycles [9]. ZnMn2O4, a binary metal oxide, stands out among TMOs for its potential in supercapacitor applications [10]. It has been extensively investigated for various applications, including photocatalytic activity, gas sensors, chemical nanosensors, and as an electrode material for different battery technologies [11,12]. ZnMn2O4 offers advantages such as a widened potential window, superior conductivity, and more active sites [13]. However, it suffers from poor electrical conductivity, which limits its rate capability, and structural instability during charge–discharge cycles, affecting its long-term performance. [14]

To address these challenges, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been introduced as a doping material to enhance the performance of ZnMn2O4-based electrodes. CQDs are zero-dimensional nanocarbon materials with particle sizes <10 nm [15]. They possess exceptional properties such as high surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, and the presence of abundant functional groups that facilitate surface functionalization. Their surface is rich in functional groups like carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups, facilitating surface modification and improving interaction with other materials [16]. The small size and high surface area of CQDs provide more active sites for electrochemical reactions. Recent research has shown that integrating CQDs into a variety of electrode materials, including metal oxides and carbon nanotubes, significantly improves electrochemical performance. For instance, CQD-incorporated composites have demonstrated improved cycling stability and specific capacitance when compared to their unmodified counterparts, demonstrating their usefulness in supercapacitor applications [15]. CQDs are highly photostable and biocompatible, making them suitable for various applications, including bioimaging and sensors [17]. Their ability to act as both electron donors and acceptors improves the electrocatalytic activity due to which CQDs highly suitable for electrochemical applications.

The doping of CQDs into ZnMn2O4 can address the shortcomings of each material, resulting in a novel material with superior properties. The introduction of CQDs significantly improves the electrical conductivity of the composite material, facilitating faster electron transfer during electrochemical reactions [15]. CQDs provide structural support to ZnMn2O4, mitigating its degradation during cycling and enhancing the overall stability of the material. The high surface area of CQDs contributes to more active sites for electrochemical reactions, improving the specific capacitance and energy density of the material. Consequently, the synergistic effects of CQDs and ZnMn2O4 result in higher specific capacitance, better rate capability, and enhanced cyclic stability [18,19].

In this study, we report the hydrothermal synthesis of bio-waste-derived zero-dimensional carbon quantum dot (CQD)-doped ZnMn₂O₄ (CQD-ZMO) using stone apple shells as a precursor for CQD. Stone apple shells are chosen due to their high carbon content, which is essential for efficient CQD formation, and their rich functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl that will enhance interaction with ZnMn₂O₄, promoting effective doping and improving electrochemical performance. Additionally, utilizing this agricultural waste aligns with sustainability goals by reducing environmental impact and contributing to a circular economy. This innovative approach effectively combines the benefits of both materials, addressing their individual limitations and resulting in a material with superior electrochemical properties. The CQD-ZMO delivers an excellent electrochemical response with 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte by delivering a high specific capacitance of ∼611 mF/cm2 at 2 mA/cm2 current density. Further, a symmetric device developed which showed an ultrahigh energy density of 0.079 mWh/cm2 at a power density of 2.46 mW/cm2. To the best of author's knowledge this is the first report of stone-apple waste derived CQD doped ZnMn2O4 for supercapacitor applications.

The chemicals zinc nitrate hexahydrate [Zn(NO3)2.6 H2O; >98 %], manganese acetate [Mn(CH3COO)2.4 H2O; >98 %], sodium hydroxide [NaOH; >99.8 %], Polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF, Mw ∼ 534,000), Potassium Hydroxide (KOH; 85 %), Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4; >98 %) polyvinylidene-difluoride (PVDF), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99.5 %), Acetylene black, and ethanol were procured from Sigma Aldrich. De-ionized (DI) water was collected from the Millipore system (resistivity: 16.2 Ω at room temperature)

The synthesis of ZnMn2O4 mixed metal oxides is achieved through a simple co-precipitation method. The procedure begins by preparing two separate solutions: one containing 1 M zinc nitrate hexahydrate and the other containing 1 M manganese acetate. These solutions are then combined in equal volumes and stirred for 2 h to ensure thorough mixing. To initiate the precipitation process, sodium hydroxide (1 M) is added slowly to the mixture. This step is performed gradually to prevent a rapid reaction and is followed by stirring the mixture overnight. The resultant precipitate is then separated from the solution using centrifugation. To remove any impurities, the precipitate is washed with acetone and subsequently dried in air. The dried precursor is then calcined at 450 °C in air to achieve the desired crystal structure and to eliminate any remaining organic compounds. This additional step enhances the purity and crystallinity of the ZnMn2O4 by promoting the rearrangement of atoms and the removal of any residual impurities.

The synthesis of CQDs stone apple shells were collected, which were then subjected to multiple cleaning steps using deionized water and ethanol. This thorough cleaning process ensures the removal of any surface contaminants and organic impurities from the shells. Following the cleaning, the shells are dried in an oven set at 110 °C overnight. This drying process removes all moisture, resulting in dry, brittle shells that are suitable for grinding. Once fully dried, the shells are ground into a fine, homogeneous powder. This powder is then mixed with a 20 wt% NaOH solution, in a volume of 60 mL and stirred for 8 h, allowing the NaOH to thoroughly impregnate the powder and interact with it. After the impregnation, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at a controlled temperature of 140 °C for a period of 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulting yellowish-brown solution was filtered to remove any residual solid particles. The filtration process ensures that the final product is free from any unreacted solid residues. The filtered yellowish-brown solution is then stored for future use.

To synthesize CQD doped ZnMn₂O₄, a simple hydrothermal method was employed. Initially, a 10 wt% CQD suspension was taken and ZnMn₂O₄ powder was added to ensure uniform distribution of CQDs within the ZnMn₂O₄ matrix. This mixture was then subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 10 h, allowing for effective integration of the CQDs into the ZnMn₂O₄ structure. After cooling, the product was centrifuged with DI water and ethanol at 5000 rpm to eliminate unreacted materials and impurities. Finally, the resulting material was dried overnight to obtain the desired CQD-doped ZnMn₂O₄. A detailed schematic representation of the synthesis procedure is provided in Fig. 1 .

The chosen loading level of 10 wt% relative to ZnMn₂O₄ strikes a balance between enhancing electrical conductivity and preventing excessive agglomeration of CQDs, which could negatively impact performance. Furthermore, this specific ratio maintains the structural stability of ZnMn₂O₄, ensuring that higher concentrations do not disrupt its integrity. By carefully selecting this loading level, the electrochemical properties of the composite will enhance while preserving the beneficial characteristics of both.

To prepare the electrodes, CQDs, ZnMn2O4, and CQD-doped ZnMn2O4 were each used as active materials. Acetylene black and PVDF were added as the conductive additive and binder, respectively, maintaining a weight ratio of 75:15:10 (active material: conductive additive: binder). A slurry was formed by adding a few drops of NMP to the mixture and then mixing for 2 h using a mortar and pestle to achieve a uniform consistency.

The graphite sheets, serving as the current collectors, were roughened with sandpaper to enhance the adhesion of the electrode material. These sheets were then cleaned thoroughly using DI water and ethanol, followed by ultrasonication to ensure the removal of any contaminants. The uniformly mixed slurry was then coated onto the pre-treated graphite sheets. The coated sheets were dried at 80 °C for 12 h to evaporate the NMP and form the final electrodes. For electrochemical analysis, the prepared electrodes were tested in a 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution, and measurements including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for a comprehensive evaluation of the capacitance, charge-discharge efficiency, and impedance characteristics of the electrodes, respectively.

Detailed information about the instrumentation and characterization techniques used in this study, including the formulae for electrochemical measurements, is provided in Supporting Information (SI) Sections I and II.

At first, the successful formation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is confirmed using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, which demonstrated distinct optical characteristics indicative of their presence. This analysis is essential for verifying the quality and properties of the synthesized CQDs, as their optical behavior is closely linked to their size and structural integrity [20]. The PL emission spectra, shown in Fig. 2a and b, reveal the variation in intensity across different excitation wavelengths starting from 360 nm to 440 nm each at band width 10 nm. The emission maximum is at ∼581 nm and the emission peaks were blue shifted from ∼581 to ∼576 nm when the excitation wavelengths were changed from 360 to 440 nm, in case of CQD as given in Fig. 2a. This shift can be attributed to the presence of sp2 clusters and a lower energy gap [21]. Further, the PL properties of the CQD-ZMO under varying excitation wavelengths showed maximum emission at ∼522 nm at 360 nm emission wavelength and shifted to ∼528 nm at 440 nm wavelength indicating red shift. Based on the PL data the CIE chromaticity coordinates of CQD and CQD-ZMO were also calculated which are critical for understanding the optical behavior are graphically represented in a consolidated graph (Fig. 2c and d) to facilitate comparative analysis. For the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the calculated chromaticity coordinates were (x = 0.473, y = 0.49) with a K value of 3067.85 which changed to (x = 0.46, y = 0.508) and K value 3334.11 upon increasing the excitation wavelength to 420 nm, in case of CQD as given in Fig. 2c. Similarly, for CQD-ZMO the chromaticity coordinates were (x = 0.318, y = 0.456) with a K value of 5883.01 and it slightly shifted to (x = 0.318, y = 0.476) with a K value of 5844.85 at higher excitation i.e. 420 nm, depicted in Fig. 2d. The inclusion of green color representation in the combined graph confirms the successful formation of CQD-ZMOs with distinct and tunable optical properties. This signifies their potential utility in applications requiring high color purity and stability, such as in supercapacitors where efficient charge storage and transfer are crucial [22]. The tunable optical properties of CQDs, as evidenced by their distinct chromaticity coordinates, suggest that they can enhance the performance of supercapacitors by improving charge transport mechanisms and energy density, thereby contributing to the development of advanced energy storage solutions [23]. Furthermore, to verify the optical properties of the synthesized CQD-ZMO, UV–vis spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectroscopy were also recorded. When exposed to UV light, the aqueous solution of CQD-ZMO emits a bright green fluorescence. For CQD-ZMO, the absorption bands are observed in the range of 250 to 300 nm of λ as shown the in Fig. 2(e). The transfer of charge between cations and lattice oxygen could be the cause of this phenomenon. The calculated band gap for CQDs-ZMO is found to be 2.03 eV.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to analyze the oxidation states of each element present in CQD-ZMO composite. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2f) reveals the presence of zinc (Zn), manganese (Mn), carbon (C), and oxygen (O), validating the successful formation of CQD-ZMO composite and potential for supercapacitor applications. The detailed convolution of each element is shown in Fig. S1 under Section I in the SI. The analysis further reveals distinct binding energy peaks for deconvoluted carbon (C1s) at 282.7 ± 0.5 eV, 284.4 ± 0.5 eV and 286.1 ± 0.5 eV, corresponding to different chemical states of carbon (Fig. S1a). The peak at 282.7 eV indicates the presence of carbonyl groups (CO), which can contribute to pseudocapacitance, thereby improving the charge storage capabilities of the supercapacitor electrode material [24]. The lower bind energy of 282.7 ± 0.5 eV in the composite is because of the charge transfer effect of CQDs with metal centres in the ZnMn2O4 makes a shift in the lower binding energy of carbon. The peak at 284.4 ± 0.5 eV is associated with sp2 hybridized carbon (CO), which is crucial for maintaining electrical conductivity; a high proportion of sp2 carbon is particularly beneficial for enhancing charge transport in supercapacitor applications [25,26]. The peak at 286.1 eV corresponds to CN bonds, which can improve the wettability and electrochemical properties of the material by facilitating ion transport during charge-discharge cycles in supercapacitors. The deconvoluted Zn 2p peaks observed at 1020.2 ± 0.5 eV and 1043.3 ± 0.5 eV correspond to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 states, respectively, indicating the existence of Zn(II) species within the structure (given in Fig. S2b) [27]. Similarly, the Mn 2p peaks at 639.2 ± 0.5 eV, 641.1 ± 0.5 eV, and 651.5 ± 0.5 eV, 653.2 ± 0.5 eV indicate the presence of both +2 and +3 oxidation states, respectively for deconvoluted peaks of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 doublet, with a spin energy separation of 12.1 eV (Fig. S1c) [10,28]. Moreover, the peaks at 642.8 ± 0.5 eV and 655.8 ± 0.5 eV indicate the presence of a +4 oxidation state, suggesting the formation of a small proportion of MnO2 in the composite. The slight shifting in Mn 2p XPS spectra is due to the variations in the binding energy of the core electrons of Mn and delocalization effect, which points to a strong interaction between ZnMn2O4 and CQDs, thus potentially enhancing the material's electrochemical performance in supercapacitor applications. Additionally, the O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1d) shows three peaks at 530.2 ± 0.5 eV, 532.5 ± 0.5 eV and 535.4 ± 0.5 eV corresponding to lattice oxygen (ZnO), OH, and COOH groups, respectively [10,29]. Therefore, these findings from the XPS analysis indicates the successful incorporation of CQDs into the ZnMn2O4 matrix.

Further, to obtain the crystallographic properties of the ZMO and CQD -ZMO, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the XRD patterns consistently show eight distinct diffraction peaks corresponding to the planes (112), (020), (013), (121), (220), (033), (231), and (224). These peaks are observed at 2θ angles of ∼28.805°, 31.88°, 34.63°, 36.62°, 47.73°, 56.74°, 59.514°, and 60.24°, respectively [13,30]. This array of peaks confirms the formation of the expected crystalline phases and highlights the material's high crystallinity. The precise identification of these peaks aligns with the spinel structure of ZnMn₂O₄ and matches well with the JCPDS Card 024–1133 [31]. Other additional peaks are attributed to ZnO and MnO2 formation. Similarly, the XRD pattern of CQD-ZMO shown in Fig. 2b indicates similar peaks with minor shifts in 2θ values and lesser intensity due to the introduction of CQD into it. Additionally, the similarity in both the pattern confirms the successful formation of the CQD-ZMO.

The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images of ZMO (Fig. 3c and d) reveal a distinct nanosheet like architecture characterized by spinel-like structures which are evident at both low and high magnifications. These spinels, synthesized via a co-precipitation method, exhibit an average width of ∼200 nm to 300 nm and possess a smooth clear surface texture that contributes to their high surface area. In contrast, the FESEM images of CQD- ZMO (Fig. 3e and f) demonstrate a similar spinel pattern but with notable morphological modifications. The incorporation of CQDs into the ZnMn2O4 matrix, achieved through mixing and subsequent hydrothermal at 180 °C, results in a more irregular and loosely structured nanosheet morphology. This alteration is determined by the formation of nanosheets, which are larger in size and exhibit rough, irregular surfaces. The unique structure of the CQD-ZMO is advantageous for supercapacitor applications, as it could provide an increased surface area that enhances charge storage and transport [32]. Additionally, the rough architecture facilitates improved ion diffusion, allowing electrolyte ions to penetrate effectively, thus enhancing the utilization of active materials and overall capacitance [33,34]. Further, an elemental mapping of CQD-ZMO is done using energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) in order to find out the homogeneity and distribution of elements like carbon (C), Zinc (Zn), Manganese (Mn), and Oxygen (O) as depicted in Fig. 3g. This well distributed elemental composition suggests that the CQDs are effectively integrated into the ZnMn₂O₄ matrix, which can enhance the conductivity.

To examine the structural and morphological characteristics of CQD-ZMO Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is done. This provides an information regarding the size, shape, and distribution of CQD within the ZnMn₂O₄ matrix and also to further assess the crystallinity of the material. Fig. 4a shows the low TEM image of CQD-ZMO where few 0D CQDs are marked in red revealing carbon spheres and it is decorated on a nanosheet which is nothing but the ZMO. A ZMO region is highlighted in green line showing thick nanosheet like morphology. Further, a TEM micrograph given in Fig. 4b matches well with the Fig. 4a description as it has similar resemblance. Then a magnified portion is given in Fig. 4c which confirms the random distribution of carbon spheres in the ZMO matrix. To visualize further, a higher magnification TEM micrograph given in Fig. 4d and e which reveals the 0D nature of CQD with an average diameter of ∼3.3 ± 0.5 nm as well as distinct lattice fringes indicating crystalline structure. The high-resolution image in Fig. 4f further illustrates individual carbon spheres marked as 1 and 2 with lattice fringes. Sphere 1 with portions highlighted in red and pink, which are later plotted in moiré patterns to measure the interplanar spacing, and it is found out to be ∼0.12 ± 0.03 nm, signifying the degree of crystallinity. Similarly, sphere 2 with portions highlighted in blue and green, plotted in moiré patterns and the interplanar spacing is measured to be ∼0.15 ± 0.02 nm. The use of moiré patterns further validates the uniform crystallinity and high quality of the CQDs, which is crucial as it enhances electrical conductivity and facilitates efficient ion transport within the material. Additionally, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern displayed in Fig. 4i shows multiple rings, confirming the polycrystalline nature of the CQD-ZMO material. This comprehensive analysis underscores the significance of TEM in elucidating the structural integrity and distribution of components within CQD-ZMO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurements in three electrode configuration
ce:section.ce:para: For electrochemical measurements of the individual electrodes consisting CQD, ZMO and CQD-ZMO as electrode material three electrode studies were done with 1 M Na2SO4 supporting electrolyte in three electrode configurations (3E). The results obtained for CQD and ZMO are given in Fig. S2, Section ii, SI while the CQD-ZMO electrochemical outcomes are given in Fig. 5 . Here, 1 M Na2SO4 was chosen due to its excellent ionic conductivity, electrochemical stability, and environmental friendliness, making it ideal for evaluating the performance of supercapacitor electrodes [35]. A comparative CV and GCD curve of CQD, ZMO and CQD-ZMO in three-electrode configuration is given in Fig.S2 (a) and (b), which indicates the superior performance of CQD-ZMO rather than the individual CQD and ZMO due to the innovative interactions between the two materials. The integration of CQDs with ZMO not only enhances conductivity but also promotes a unique electron transfer mechanism that allows for rapid charge accumulation. As a result, the CQD-ZMO composite not only addresses the limitations of each individual component but also opens new avenues for enhanced performance in energy storage applications to its synergistic effects. Detailed electrochemical performance of CQD and ZMO are their comparison is discussed in SI.

Fig. 5a displays the CV curves of CQD-ZMO electrodes at various scan rates within a potential window of 0 to 0.9 V. This analysis was performed to assess the electrochemical behavior of the material. The absence of prominent redox peaks in the CV curves indicates that the charge storage mechanism is primarily capacitive rather than involving faradaic processes. The smooth, nearly rectangular shape of the CV curves indicates high reversibility and excellent capacitive behavior. This aligns with the GCD results, which also showed smooth, triangular discharge profiles as given in Fig. 5b. The charge storage process begins with EDLC, where ions from the electrolyte accumulate at the electrode surface upon voltage application. This electrostatic interaction forms an electric double layer, allowing for rapid charge storage. In parallel, pseudocapacitance contributes significantly to the overall capacitance through fast, reversible faradaic redox reactions at the electrode surface. During charging, Zn2+ ions intercalate into the ZnMn₂O₄ lattice, which not only provides additional charge storage but also enhances electrochemical performance due to the rapid kinetics of these redox processes. This dual mechanism allows for a substantial increase in specific capacitance compared to systems relying solely on EDLC [35,36]. Additionally, the use of 1 M Na₂SO₄ as an electrolyte improves ionic conductivity, enabling efficient ion transport to and from the electrode surface during cycling. Further, detailed specific capacitances were calculated from the GCD curves of each electrode in order to compare the performance shown in Fig. 5c. At a current density of 2 mA/cm2, the specific capacitances are ∼219 mF/cm2 for CQD electrodes, ∼336 mF/cm2 for ZMO electrodes, and ∼ 611 mF/cm2 for CQD-ZMO electrodes. The CQD electrodes exhibit the lowest capacitance, reflecting their reliance on electrical double-layer capacitance, which limits their charge storage capacity [15,19]. Conversely, ZMO electrodes achieve a higher capacitance due to the pseudocapacitive behavior of ZMO, where redox reactions contribute significantly to charge storage. The CQD-ZMO electrodes show the highest specific capacitance, underscoring the effective integration of CQDs with ZMO.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to investigate the frequency-dependent behavior of CQD, ZMO, and CQD-ZMO. The Bode plot shown in Fig. 5e, comprising both magnitude and phase components, which provides insights into the materials' impedance characteristics across a frequency range of 1 Hz to 100 kHz. The Bode magnitude plot reveals the materials' capacitive or resistive behavior, with slopes indicating the dominance of capacitive effects at lower frequencies, which is crucial for supercapacitor applications. The Bode phase plot illustrates the phase angle response, highlighting the frequency at which maximum capacitive behavior occurs, thus informing on the charge storage capabilities of the materials [37,38]. Complementing the Bode analysis, the Nyquist plot (Fig. 5d) offers a visual representation of the electrochemical processes occurring at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The Nyquist plots for CQD, ZMO, and CQD-ZMO exhibit semicircular arcs, where the high-frequency intercept corresponds to the solution resistance (Rs) and the semicircle diameter indicates the charge transfer resistance (Rct). The Rs values were measured at ∼1.25 Ω for CQD, ∼2.21 Ω for CQD-ZMO, and ∼4.2 Ω for ZMO, suggesting that the incorporation of CQDs decreases the solution resistance. However, the Rct values reveal a more pronounced trend, with ZMO exhibiting the highest charge transfer resistance of ∼0.6 Ω, while CQD and CQD-ZMO demonstrated significantly lower Rct values of ∼0.23 Ω and ∼0.42 Ω, respectively. This reduction in charge transfer resistance in CQD-doped materials indicates enhanced charge transfer kinetics that can be attributed to the improved electrical conductivity and additional active sites provided by CQDs. Overall, the combined analysis of Bode and Nyquist plots confirms the favorable electrochemical performance of CQD-ZMO in supercapacitor applications, highlighting the benefits of integrating carbon quantum dots into the ZnMn2O4 matrix for enhanced energy storage capabilities.

In the pursuit of advancing energy storage technologies, a full-cell symmetric supercapacitor was developed using CQD-ZMO as the active material, with a 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. The device was designed to optimize performance within a voltage window of 1.2 V. The electrodes, composed of CQD-ZMO, were prepared to ensure uniformity and effective charge storage capabilities. The CV tests revealed well-defined oxidation and reduction peaks (Fig. 6a), while the GCD plots are also indicative of the pseudocapacitive behavior of CQD-ZMO (Fig. 6b), with specific capacitance values reaching 396.2 mF/cm2 at a low current density of 2 mA/cm2. As the current density increased, the specific capacitance demonstrated a gradual decrease, which is a common characteristic in supercapacitor materials, confirming the robustness of the CQD-ZMO under varying operational conditions [5]. The GCD profiles further corroborated the ideal pseudocapacitive behavior, exhibiting non-linear discharge curves that maintained their shape even at higher current densities, underscoring the material's capability to sustain energy storage and release characteristics. Further, the symmetric supercapacitor exhibited an IR drop of 0.04 V at a current density of 2 mA/cm2, which increased to 0.168 V at 10 mA/cm2, as shown in Fig. 6c. This increase in IR drop can be attributed to several factors. First, while the incorporation of carbon quantum dots enhances the electrical conductivity of the CQD-ZMO, higher current densities can lead to insufficient time for ions to diffuse and interact fully within the porous structure, resulting in increased resistance [39–41]. Second, the contact between the CQD-ZMO electrode and the 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte facilitates ion transport, but the effectiveness of this interface can diminish at higher current densities due to the increased demand for ion movement. Additionally, EIS was utilized to investigate the frequency-dependent behavior of the device over a frequency range of 1 to 100 Hz. The Nyquist plot (Fig. 6d) further illustrated the electrochemical processes at the electrode-electrolyte interface, displaying a semicircular arc. The Rct was measured at 1.3 Ω, indicating efficient charge transfer kinetics within the device.

The Ragone plot is shown to evaluate the performance of device by illustrating the relationship between specific energy (energy density) and specific power (power density). The CQD-ZMO//CQD-ZMO symmetric supercapacitor device demonstrated an ultrahigh energy density of 0.079 mWh/cm2 at a power density of 2.46 mW/cm2, given in Fig. 6e. This performance indicates a favorable balance between energy storage capacity and rapid discharge capabilities, making it suitable for applications requiring both high energy and power. Furthermore, the device demonstrated excellent cycling stability, exhibiting a remarkable 95.2 % capacitance retention over 5000 cycles at a current density of 10 mA/cm2 as shown in Fig. 6f. The inherent reasons attributed to this remarkable performance of the device could be (i) enhanced conductivity: the incorporation of CQDs into ZnMnO₂ significantly improves electrical conductivity, facilitating efficient charge transport during cycling [42]. (ii) Structural Integrity: CQDs provide mechanical support to the electrode, preventing particle agglomeration and preserving the active surface area needed for electrochemical reactions [43]. (iii) Dual charge storage mechanism: the supercapacitor utilizes both electric double-layer capacitance (EDLC) from CQD and pseudocapacitance from ZnMnO₂. EDLC arises from the electrostatic charge accumulation at the electrode/electrolyte interface, while pseudocapacitance involves fast redox reactions at the surface of ZnMnO₂. This combination enhances overall capacitance and allows for efficient charge/discharge cycles, contributing to long-term cycling stability [44,45]. (iv) Degradation prevention: the structural integrity provided by CQDs helps to mitigate common degradation mechanisms such as particle agglomeration and electrolyte evaporation. CQDs reinforce the electrode matrix, maintaining the active surface area and preventing capacity loss over extended cycling. Additionally, the stable chemical environment created by the 1 M Na₂SO₄ electrolyte further reduces the likelihood of electrolyte decomposition and offers good ionic conductivity thereby maintaining consistent ion transport, enhancing overall device longevity [44,46]. Lastly (v) effective current collecter: the use of graphite paper as a current collector minimizes interface resistance, ensuring efficient electron transport and reducing energy losses [24].

In addition to this, the degradation mechanisms of CQD-ZMO electrode over cycling involve several interconnected factors that can significantly affect their performance. At first, the mechanical stress from repeated charge-discharge cycles can lead to micro-cracking, compromising the material's structural integrity. This is often compounded by electrochemical corrosion, where interactions with the 1 M Na₂SO₄ electrolyte facilitate reactions that further degrade the electrode. Additionally, as ions intercalate and deintercalate during cycling, they induce local strain within the crystal lattice, potentially causing phase changes that increase electrical resistance. Over time, salt deposits may form on the electrode surface due to evaporation or concentration gradients in the electrolyte, hindering ion transport and reducing the effective surface area for electrochemical reactions. Detailed information about the structural and morphological change of the electrode after stability is given in SI Section iii.

The comparison of electrochemical performance among QD-based materials for SC is given in Table 1 . A recent report on CQDs/graphene microfibers-based supercapcitors delivered a specific capacitance of 215 mF cm2 at 1 mA cm2 with EMIBF4/PVDF-HFP ionic electrolyte. Though the electrochemical performance is good considering the use of ionic electrolyte, the materials used here include a complex synthesis procedure including modified Hummer's method and magnetic hyperthermia method along with two-stage heating by keeping the material in PTFE tube to obtain the desired CQD/graphene microfibers [47]. Another drawback is that graphene can easily oxidize during the microfiber formation, which would potentially affect the stability of the devices over a long period of time. Liu et al. developed two different reports, i.e., Au-GQD//Au-PANI nanofibers [48] and Au-GQD//Au-MnO2 [49] asymmetric micro-supercapacitors fabricated via electrodeposition on interdigital finger Au electrodes through DC power supply of 80 V for 30 min. Even though the fabrication methods are novel, the overall efficacy of these devices is still restricted, as evidenced by the average electrochemical performance results. This emphasizes the need for substitute materials or techniques that can provide microfiber applications with improved performance and cost-effectiveness. Another report on flexible solid-state supercapacitor by utilizing CQD-PPy nanocomposite as electrode material with LiCl-PVA electrolyte. The device delivered 315 mF/cm2 of specific capacitance at 0.2 mA/cm2 current density and 85.7 % capacitance retention over 2000 cycles at 2 mA/cm2 [50]. Here, the interaction between the LiCl-PVA gel electrolyte and the CQDs/PPy composite may not be optimal, potentially leading to reduced ionic conductivity or increased resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Also, the long-term cycling stability raises concerns about material fatigue, particularly in flexible applications where bending and stretching could exacerbate mechanical stresses on the electrode materials, leading to cracks or delamination over time. Then, a report on ZnO/ZnMn2O4/rGO composite-based supercapacitor delivered a good electrochemical performance of 0.098 mWh/cm2 energy density with a corresponding power density of 1.6 mW/cm2 with 6 M KOH electrolyte [51]. Here, the ZnO/ZnMn₂O₄ did not perform optimally as a supercapacitor due to its limited conductivity and slower ion transport, which hindered its overall charge storage capabilities. In contrast, the incorporation of rGO into the composite significantly enhanced performance by improving electrical conductivity and providing a larger surface area for charge storage. The rGO facilitates faster electron and ion transport, enabling both EDLC and pseudocapacitive mechanisms to work synergistically. Considering this, we developed for the first time bio-waste derived CQD doped ZnMn2O4 via low-cost facile hydrothermal and co-precipitation method. The nanosheet like morphology of ZMO with uniformly distributed CQDs all over it has an excellent conductivity thereby resulting superior electrochemical performance in neutral medium. The CQD-ZMO//CQD-ZMO exhibits an impressive energy density of 0.079 mWh/cm2 at a power density of 2.46 mW/cm2, indicating its capability for rapid energy storage and release. Furthermore, the symmetric device also shows excellent stability i.e. 95.2 % capacitance retention after 5000 cycles, suggesting its long-term reliability. The incorporation of CQDs into the ZnMn₂O₄ matrix not only enhances specific capacitance but also improves charge transfer and diffusion characteristics within the electrode material. This is consistent with findings from other studies that highlight the benefits of hybrid materials in enhancing electrochemical performance through increased conductivity. Hence, this work presents a significant advancement in supercapacitor technology, offering superior performance and stability.

In conclusion, this study presents a novel approach for the synthesis of bio-waste derived 0D CQD doped ZnMn2O4 (CQD-ZMO) material through simple hydrothermal treatment, highlighting its potential for advanced supercapacitor applications. The CQD-ZMO has well defined polycrystalline nature with nanosheets like surface morphology incorporated with CQD spheres. It demonstrates exceptional electrochemical performance in the three-electrode arrangement, achieving a peak specific capacitance of 611 mF/cm2 at 2 mA/cm2 in 0 to 0.9 V of potential window of with 1 M Na2SO4 supporting electrolyte. Furthermore, the symmetric supercapacitor device, utilizing the CQD-ZMO//CQD-ZMO configuration, exhibits outstanding characteristics, attaining an ultrahigh energy density of 0.079 mWh/cm2 at a power density of 2.46 mW/cm2. The CQD-doped ZnMn2O4 supercapacitor not only offer enhanced energy and power densities but also provide stability and cost-effectiveness, paving the way for the development of more efficient and sustainable energy storage solutions for the future.

Om Priya Nanda: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sushmee Badhulika: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
